findings.title: CrashHandler uses implicit launch of CrashReportActivity without explicit FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK / FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK discipline documented
findings.description: Tech-spec Flow A step 3c uses `startActivity(Intent(ctx, CrashReportActivity::class.java).addFlags(NEW_TASK))`. That is an explicit-component Intent (fully qualified class), which combined with `android:exported="false"`, `android:taskAffinity=""`, `excludeFromRecents="true"`, and the absence of any intent-filter, correctly prevents third-party apps from launching CrashReportActivity. Residual concern: because `taskAffinity` is empty-string and `NEW_TASK` is used, a pre-existing task (e.g. remnants of previous crash) could in rare OEM cases be reused. Spec does not explicitly add `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK` or `FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_DOCUMENT`, so there is a theoretical but not practical path where stale CrashReportActivity instances stack. No arbitrary-intent-launch vector exists (no exported surface, no PendingIntent exposure). Recommend adding `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK | FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK` to be defensive. The configuration (exported=false, no intent-filter, taskAffinity='', excludeFromRecents) is otherwise adequate against arbitrary-intent launch.
findings.location: Section: Architecture — Flow A step 3c; AndroidManifest.xml line declaring CrashReportActivity
findings.impact: Low practical impact — attacker cannot reach CrashReportActivity from another app (exported=false, no intent-filter). Only internal robustness concern around task reuse.
findings.recommendation: Use `addFlags(Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK or Intent.FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TASK)` in CrashHandler to guarantee a fresh task stack. Optionally add an explicit `setPackage(ctx.packageName)` on the Intent as belt-and-suspenders against any framework quirk routing to another resolvable component (theoretical only — explicit ComponentName already pins it).

findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: Handler inner-failure catch silently kills process with no breadcrumb
findings.description: Decision in Flow A step 4: 'Any Throwable inside the handler → outer catch → Process.killProcess without retrying any step.' This is correct to prevent bootstrap loops (per Risk row 1), but the failure is completely silent — no ErrorLog.e, no Android.util.Log.e, no marker file. If the handler consistently fails (e.g. filesDir full, SELinux denies write), every crash will be a silent kill and the user will see only the system 'app stopped' dialog, with no clue that diagnostics failed. Because ErrorLog.e is suspend and unsafe here (correctly excluded per Decision 10), a safer breadcrumb is a `android.util.Log.e("CrashHandler", "handler failed", t)` inside the outer catch — this writes to logcat which can later be captured by the About-export path in a subsequent run. Tech-spec's CrashHandlerTest does assert 'killer called exactly once, no re-entry' but does not assert any diagnostic emission on failure.
findings.location: Section: Architecture — Flow A step 4; Decision 10; Task 2 CrashHandlerTest
findings.impact: Loss of diagnostic observability in exactly the scenario users need help most. Undermines the feature's core purpose (US-C proactive export can still miss the reason crash.log is empty).
findings.recommendation: In CrashHandler's outer `catch (Throwable)`, emit a single `android.util.Log.e(TAG, "crash-handler self-failed", t)` before `Process.killProcess`. This is non-suspend, synchronous, and uses no Kotlin coroutine machinery — safe in an uncaught context. It lands in logcat which the About-export captures on next run, giving a recovery path. Add a unit test asserting the log emission.

findings.title: ProcessBuilder logcat args are effectively untainted
findings.description: The command `ProcessBuilder("logcat", "-d", "-v", "threadtime", "--pid=${Process.myPid()}", "*:E")` is invoked via ProcessBuilder's argv form (list of strings), not via a shell. All args except `--pid=<N>` are compile-time string literals. The only interpolation is `Process.myPid()`, which is an `int` returned from the Android SDK — not attacker-controllable from any external surface. No `Runtime.exec(String)` single-string form is used, so shell meta-characters cannot apply even hypothetically. This is safe against command injection. The finding is informational / confirm-only.
findings.location: Section: Architecture — LogcatReader description; Task 3 LogcatReader.kt
findings.impact: None — no tainted input reaches ProcessBuilder.
findings.recommendation: Keep the list-form ProcessBuilder constructor. Add a unit-test assertion in LogcatReaderTest that the command list has exactly the expected elements and that the fifth element starts with the literal prefix `--pid=` followed only by decimal digits (guards against future refactors that might concatenate other input).

findings.title: SAF openOutputStream result requires null-check and stream-close discipline; spec relies on .use
findings.description: Task 3 says 'writeTo(uri: Uri, content: String) using ContentResolver.openOutputStream(uri).use'. ContentResolver.openOutputStream can return null on some edge cases (bogus provider, revoked grant mid-flight). `.use` on a null receiver would NPE before the block executes. The user-selected URI cannot be forced to point at attacker-controlled content (user picked it via SAF system UI), so there is no confused-deputy risk — the app has no persistent URI permission and only holds a transient write grant from the SAF result. No need to validate scheme, authority, or path beyond non-null. However, handling null returned from openOutputStream should be explicit (not via NPE) to satisfy the 'IOException → Toast Не удалось сохранить лог' AC cleanly.
findings.location: Section: Implementation Tasks — Task 3 writeTo(); user-spec AC 'Ошибка записи ... показывает Toast'
findings.impact: Null-return would surface as NullPointerException caught by a broad `catch` or crash if not caught — breaks the 'IOException-only' AC path. No confidentiality/integrity impact.
findings.recommendation: Implement as `contentResolver.openOutputStream(uri)?.use { it.write(content.toByteArray(UTF_8)) } ?: throw IOException("openOutputStream returned null")`. Add a unit-test case for the null-return path (mock ContentResolver to return null).

findings.title: Ordering of Thread.setDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler before DefaultDownloadRepository.mainActivityFqn
findings.description: Decision 4 installs the handler before `DefaultDownloadRepository.mainActivityFqn = …`. Intent is to catch crashes in that very assignment line. The install itself is a single atomic assignment on the JVM-global static; no in-process attacker can race it because no app code runs before Application.onCreate. Third-party-thread crashes (e.g. a framework-spawned thread during ContentProvider init that runs even earlier, per Android init order ContentProvider → Application.onCreate) are NOT caught — but this is a known Android-platform limitation, not a design flaw of this tech-spec. No external actor can exploit the pre-handler window because there is no external surface at that lifecycle phase. Confirming there is no ordering flaw to exploit. Flag only to note that ContentProvider crashes during ContentProvider#onCreate run before setDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler — PhoneWrap uses Hilt and likely has no custom ContentProvider with complex logic, but if WorkManager or Hilt-generated providers throw, they bypass the handler.
findings.impact: Very narrow pre-handler window exists for ContentProvider-phase crashes; not exploitable by any external actor, but reduces diagnostic coverage for Hilt/WorkManager init failures.
findings.recommendation: Document the known limitation in NOTES.md or tech-spec Risks: 'Crashes in ContentProvider#onCreate (runs before Application.onCreate per Android lifecycle) bypass CrashHandler.' No code change needed. If critical, consider adding a minimal bootstrap handler via androidx.startup Initializer with priority=FIRST — but this is overkill for Phase 2.5.

findings.title: Data leaked in exported .txt is appropriate to stated threat model; no PII redaction needed for tester→dev channel
findings.description: Exported contents: device manufacturer/model (Honor / HONOR 200), Android version, RAM totals, applicationId, versionName/Code, debug flag, full stacktraces (crash.log, no truncation of content except 100 KB head-cap), logcat filtered to own-pid level ERROR, list of downloaded model ids and sizes, active model id. No filesystem paths beyond package-scoped `filesDir/logs/`. No user chat content, no prompts, no credentials (app has none — fully local LLM per CLAUDE.md). The user-spec explicitly states 'на фазе закрытого тестирования фильтрация содержимого не применяется — приоритет у полноты диагностики' and NOTES.md backlog tracks Phase 5 review before public release. Threat model is tester→dev over messenger, user-initiated SAF save (explicit consent). Android model/manufacturer is not a unique identifier and is visible to every installed app via Build constants anyway. Logcat ERROR lines could theoretically contain third-party component IDs or file paths, but own-pid filter limits blast radius. Data exposure is acknowledged appropriately. Finding is a confirm-only note, not a gap.
findings.location: Section: Data Models — Exported .txt layout; user-spec Ограничения 'Приватность'
findings.impact: Low — user-controlled SAF save, tester-dev channel, no credentials/PII in app. Phase 5 will revisit per NOTES.md.
findings.recommendation: No action for Phase 2.5. In Phase 5, before public release, implement logcat-line regex filter to strip anything matching `/data/user/\d+/[^/]+/` paths (per-user file paths) and potentially IMEI/serial patterns. Ensure `BuildConfig.DEBUG` flag wrapping of dev-gesture is tracked (already in NOTES.md).

findings.title: Dev-gesture in release builds is low-risk but warrants one additional guard
findings.description: Decision 11 allows the 7-tap gesture in release builds for Phase 2.5, justified by identical APK semantics between dev and tester. Abuse analysis: a regular user who discovers the gesture can crash the app (denial of self-service — only affects own install), generate a crash.log locally, and optionally SAF-export a diagnostic file containing their own device info. They cannot: elevate privilege, read other apps' data, reach the crash activity from another app (exported=false), or exfiltrate anything not already visible to their own installed apps. Worst case: a curious user fills storage with saved .txt files — but SAF is user-initiated each time. The crash dialog itself requires 7 discrete taps within a 2-s-per-gap window, not accidentally triggerable by normal scroll/navigation. This is low-risk for Phase 2.5. Phase 5 BuildConfig.DEBUG wrap is already tracked in NOTES.md.
findings.impact: Negligible in closed-testing phase. Self-DOS only; no cross-app or cross-user impact.
findings.recommendation: Add a secondary confirmation requirement for the release path: the AlertDialog already asks 'Спровоцировать тест-краш?' — this is adequate. No code change needed for Phase 2.5. Verify NOTES.md explicitly lists the Phase 5 BuildConfig.DEBUG wrap as a release blocker (per spec this is already done).
